blurb: Colorado's tough native wild grape: a vigorous tendril-climber with handsome lobed leaves, clusters of tart blue-black fruit for jelly and the birds, and clear-yellow fall color. Plant it where it can ramble.
water: Moderate; adaptable but appreciates some moisture (often riparian in the wild)
spread: Vigorous woody climber by forked tendrils; can be aggressive — prune to control
seasons: Fragrant greenish flowers in spring; bold lobed foliage in summer; tart blue-black fruit clusters in late summer; clear-yellow fall color; bare woody vine in winter
wildlife: Fruit feeds many birds and mammals; larval host for sphinx moths; dense nesting cover
toxic: Non-toxic; the fruit and young leaves are edible (see Edible parts)

care.hardiness: Exceptionally cold hardy — rated to roughly USDA zone 2–3 and native across the northern Plains and Rockies — so Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters pose no risk; no winter protection is needed.[1][3]
care.planting: Plant dormant or container stock in spring after the soil warms, or in early fall, siting it at the foot of a stout arbor, fence, or trellis it can climb. Give it room — it is a large, woody vine. In a pot it dries fast and its roots are less cold-hardy, so use a big container, water often, and sink or shelter the pot over winter; it performs far better planted in the ground.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade; it fruits and colors best in full sun but tolerates the dappled shade of its native woodland edges and streambanks.[1][3]
care.soil: Very adaptable to the region's soils, including alkaline clay; it grows naturally on riverbanks and floodplains in moist, fertile ground but handles a range of textures as long as drainage is reasonable.[1][3]
care.water: Moderate. Though a riparian native that appreciates summer moisture, it is fairly drought-tolerant once its deep roots are established; in our semi-arid climate a deep soak in the heat of summer keeps foliage and fruit at their best.[1][3]
care.spacing: A single vine covers a large arbor or fence run, so space plants about 8 ft apart along a support, more for a screen; expect 15–30+ ft of vigorous, spreading growth.[1][3]
care.propagation: Grows readily from seed and from cuttings. Clean seed from the ripe fruit and give it a cold-moist stratification (about 2–3 months at ~34–40°F) to break dormancy before spring sowing. The easy non-seed routes are dormant hardwood cuttings of the previous season's wood taken in late winter, and layering, where a low cane pinned to the soil roots on its own.[1]
care.bloom: Small, fragrant greenish flowers open in late spring, before the fruit sets; they are inconspicuous but a minor early nectar source.[1][3]
care.maintenance: Prune hard while fully dormant in late winter — the vine 'bleeds' sap heavily if cut in early spring as the sap rises, so finish pruning before bud swell. Cut back the previous year's growth to a framework of strong canes to keep the vine in bounds and productive, and remove tangled or dead wood. It can grow aggressively, so expect to prune yearly.[1][3]
care.troubles: Generally tough and trouble-free; can attract leafhoppers, Japanese beetles, and the grape forms of powdery and downy mildew, none usually serious on a wildlife vine. Birds compete heavily for the ripe fruit — net if you want a harvest.[1][3]
care.harvest: Fruit ripens to blue-black in late summer into fall and is sourest before frost, sweetening after the first frosts. Pick fully colored clusters; the tart, seedy berries are best cooked down — strain for juice, jelly, syrup, or wine — though they can be eaten fresh once dead-ripe. Young leaves can be picked in spring while tender.[2][3][4]
care.companions: Pair it with other tough natives in a wildlife planting — let it climb a chokecherry or serviceberry, a sturdy fence, or an arbor, with native grasses and forbs below; keep it off shrubs you don't want overtopped, as it is vigorous.[1]

edible.card: Ripe fruit (jelly, juice, wine) & young leaves
edible.summary: A genuine native wild edible: the ripe blue-black fruit makes excellent jelly, juice, and wine, and the young leaves are used like cultivated grape leaves.[2][3][4]
edible.parts: Two parts are eaten. The ripe fruit — small, blue-black, with a heavy bloom — is sour until the first frosts, after which it sweetens; it may be eaten fresh but is far better cooked. The tender young leaves, picked in spring, are eaten like the grape leaves of commerce. Native peoples ate the fruit fresh and dried it for winter, and riverbank grape was a documented food plant across its range.[2][4]
edible.preparation: Because the fruit is tart and seedy, it is best cooked: simmer and strain it for jelly, juice, syrup, or wine, or wait for fully frost-sweetened berries to eat fresh. Young leaves are blanched and used as wraps (dolma-style) like other grape leaves. No detoxification is needed — only ordinary cooking.[2][3][4]
edible.caution: Identify with certainty before eating. The most dangerous look-alike is Canada moonseed (Menispermum canadense), a poisonous vine whose blue-black grape-like fruit grows in similar clusters; the reliable tell is the seed — true grape has 2–4 rounded, pear-shaped seeds per berry, while moonseed contains a single flattened, crescent- or moon-shaped seed. If a 'grape' has one crescent seed, do not eat it. Also note the raw fruit is very tart, acidic, and seedy and is best cooked; eat only fully ripe, frost-sweetened berries fresh.[3][4]
